cards.back: A tool built into your IDE that lets you pause program execution, examine variables, and step through code line-by-line without modifying source code.

cards.text: The three main stepping commands are {{c1::step into}}, {{c2::step over}}, and {{c3::step out}}.

cards.front: What does 'step into' do in a debugger?
cards.back: Executes the next statement. If it's a function call, enters (steps into) that function so you can debug it line-by-line.

cards.front: What does 'step over' do in a debugger?
cards.back: Executes the next statement. If it's a function call, executes the entire function without stepping into it.

cards.front: What does 'step out' do in a debugger?
cards.back: Executes the rest of the current function and returns to the caller, stopping at the next statement after the function call.

cards.text: To use a debugger effectively, compile your program in {{c1::debug}} mode, which includes debugging {{c2::symbols}} and disables {{c3::optimizations}}.

cards.concept: When to use each stepping command
cards.explanation:
• Step into: When you want to debug inside a function to see its execution
  • Step over: When you trust a function works and just want to execute it
  • Step out: When you're done debugging a function and want to return to caller
examples: std::cout << x; // Step over: just execute it
// In deep function: step out to return

cards.front: Why should you avoid stepping into standard library functions?
cards.back: Standard library functions are well-tested and stepping into them clutters your debugging session. Use step over instead.

cards.text: When the debugger is paused, you can {{c1::inspect}} variables, examine the {{c2::call stack}}, and even {{c3::modify}} values.

cards.back: Compiler optimizations can reorder code, eliminate variables, or inline functions, making the debugger's view not match your source code.
Step into - Execute next line and enter functions
Step over - Execute next line without entering functions

cards.concept: Advantages of integrated debugger over print statements
cards.explanation:
• No source code modifications needed
  • Can inspect any variable at any time
  • Can step through execution line by line
  • Can view call stack and execution history
  • Can modify variables to test fixes
  • More powerful for complex bugs

cards.text: Debug builds include {{c1::debug symbols}} and disable {{c2::optimizations}} so the debugger view matches your source code.

cards.question: Which stepping command would you use here and why?
cards.answer: Use step over on the trusted_library_function() call (don't need to debug it), then step into on the process() call.
cards.explanation: Step over trusted code to save time, step into code you need to debug.
